A colorful children's game in which hedgehogs try to collect the best fruit and avoid spoiled plums. The players occupy the edges of rows onto which fruit tiles are placed. Once a row is filled up, a player occupying it can grab the tiles and collect points; if the series in a row is a particular configuration (e.g. all fruit of the same type or color) it counts for a higher score. All players can place tiles in all rows, however, and sabotage each other's harvest.

Łamisłówkais a tile-placement word game played on a field of hexes clustered around numbered fields, where players must place words in twisted lines and the situation on the board is constantly shifting, and can be subject to interpretation. Somewhat reminiscent of diagramless crosswords.

Each player puts down tiles that, in connection to some already on the board, spell out a word; the word has to be declared, as, unlike scrabble, it could be read in many directions, touches letters that aren't part of the word, and so on. Each letter has an assigned number of points, and each word places scores points. Additionally, if one closes the right surrounding a numbered field, one collects as many points as the number signifies. The player with the highest score at the end of the game wins.

boardgame: The Book of Prehistoric Pop-up Board Games
description:
Simple roll dice and move games with a dinosaur theme, in the form of a pop-up book with four three-dimensional boards, a set of paper pawns for each, a spinning dial and an informational booklet.

Very visually attractive and easy to grab for travel, it's a perfect distraction outside the house.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
In Mini Prix, you are both team manager & driver for your team. The object of the game is that each player should try to qualify his cars as far up the grid as possible & then beat all the other cars in the race. The winner is the first car to cross the finish line.

A motor racing roll and move with a push your luck element.
Using 3d6 to determine your speed off the line, you can then accelerate & decelerate during your turn in order to safely negotiate the track. Attempting to take a corner at too high a speed results in rolling the dice against your car's current roadworthiness which can result in further damage or even crashing out of the race.

boardgame: 精霊回路ドライヴ (Seirei Kairo Drive)
description:
User summary
In this cooperative game, players begin by selecting hero cards from among four different colors (representing the elements earth, wind, fire, and water) to form a party. Each player then receives a hand of cards drawn from a central deck, which also represents the players' hit points. The players face a series of monster cards drawn from an enemy deck.

The heroes begin at level one, but they earn tokens during the game that may be spent to level up their attack abilities. Each hero may only attack when the player has played a card matching its color. The card that is played must match either the color or the number of the card that was previously played, though multiple cards may be played under special circumstances.

Monster attacks force players to discard from the central deck, decreasing the available hit points. Leveling up the heroes helps players to reclaim these discarded hit points, essentially "healing" the group.

After facing a series of monsters, the players confront a powerful boss monster in a final battle. The players all win or lose together.

Publisher's summary
The players choose unit cards of various spirits to form parties. Each unit card has unique effects and can be played in various combinations.

Holding circuit cards with numbers 0 to 4 and 4 colors as your hands, play the cards with numbers and colors matching those of the cards in the play area. If you have a unit card matching the played card in your party, that unit will attack the enemy.

Thus, play cards to attack and destroy the enemies that appear one after another. You can also gain magical power by card plays and use that power to
activate your unit's special ability as well as raise the unit's level to lead the battle advantageously. The enemies' final boss is very powerful. The players need to cooperate and support each other for victory. In this 3rd edition, the statuses of unit cards and enemy cards have been adjusted and the easy mode has been introduced.

プレイヤーは様々な精霊のユニットカードを何枚か選んでパーティーを結成します。
ユニットカードはそれぞれ固有の効果を持っていて、様々な組み合わせで遊べます。

ゲームでは、0～4の数字と４つの色が描かれた回路カードを手札に持ち、場札の数字や色と同じものをプレイします。
プレイしたカードと同じ色のユニットカードが自分のパーティーにいれば、そのユニットが敵に攻撃を行います。

そうやってカードをプレイして、次々と現れる敵に攻撃を行って撃破していきます。 
またカードプレイによって魔力を獲得でき、その魔力を使ってユニットごとの特殊能力を使ったりユニット自身のレベルを上げていくことで戦いを有利に展開できます。
最後に登場する敵ボスキャラクターは非常に強力で、プレイヤー同士で協力・サポートし、力を合わせて勝利を目指してください。
この第３版では、ユニットカードと敵カードのステータス調整、イージーモードの導入などが行われています。
artists: Jewel Saver FREE (ジュエルセイバーFREE)

description:
Myrtille is a co-operative game trying to raise awareness of environmental issues. Players will build and explore the small world of Myrtille and complete missions what can have some environmental impact.

The game is made of location card (river, house, beach, forest, garbage dump, shop…), colored pawns, objective cards (which indicates what pawns should go where), two dice (one with colors and one with numbers), and time cards.

Each player has one objective card and a few location cards. The four edges of each location cards have one of three colors.

At the start of the game, one location card is put on the table and will represent the board used by all the players. All colored pawns are placed on this card.

On its turn, a player rolls the two die, and uses only one of them.

If they choose the colored dice, then they add a location card to the board, that will thus get bigger and bigger. To do so, they have to place one of their location card next to a card already on the board such that both adjacent edges have the same color as the die. Also, if the new card is adjacent to more than one card, all adjacent edges must have the same color.

If they choose the numbered dice, then they can move one pawn using as many movement points as shown on the dice, each adjacent card using one movement point. They can only move a pawn whose color matches one of the pawn depicted on their objective card.

An objective card is complete when all pawns depicted on the objective card are on one location card matching the location of the objective card. The group as a whole earns the victory points printed on the objective card.

The game has a limited number of turn, which is tracked using the time cards.

Pittle is a card drafting and tile placement game where each player tries to complete color patterns on their secret cards for points.

Each player receives an equal number of color tiles. They are then dealt 4 cards from the secret card deck. Each player selects one card and passes the others, continuing like this until everyone has a hand of 4 cards.

The first player then plays a colored tile in the center of the board. Going clockwise, each player plays one colored tile anywhere on the board until all tiles have been played.

Once the final tile has been played, players reveal their secret cards which depict color patterns which have been completed on the board. Points for completed cards are totaled for each player and the player with the highest point total wins.

In Juris Perjurious, players are wealthy Roman citizens who have gathered at the city forum to discuss new laws. Players are dealt a hidden support card representing an influential citizen who supports their claims. Your goal is to be the first player to collect 11 coins and get your new law approved. Each turn you may trade away your support card or use individual support card powers. Using the ability of a support card is the only way to collect coins needed for victory. Players will claim they have support of various citizens to gain coins, look at other player's cards or they may trade coins or cards, it will be up to all players to decide who may be bluffing. Players can only be stopped by making a public challenge of their support. If you are wrong a small fine is paid to the city Quaestor.

Kogenercja is a game created per request of PGNiG SA - the largest Polish oil and gas exploration and production company.

The players compete in order to create the most efficient thermal-electric power plant. To improve their business, they gather resources needed for the growth of such company and building a power plant. In this game knowledge is as important as an understanding of economy, as players have to answer quiz-type questions in order to advance in the game.

As a game for the entire family, Kogeneracja promotes knowledge about the ways of producing energy and how power plants work.

And Away is a Tabletop Novel, a solitaire card game.

It is the story of The Wanderer, who is stuck on a desert planet learning Kung-Fu from various teachers, some of which are not human, some of which are very much just a skull, or maybe even a cactus.  Above the desert is a green verdant moon called Paradise.  The Wanderer is learning Kung-Fu in hopes to somehow defy the impossible and reach this moon.

In the desert player’s will find familiarities to the world of Spell Saga.  As if the same world had two reflections.


From the Introduction:

Welcome to the world of And Away: ~ Tabletop Novel~

This is a card game for anyone to play alone.  It contains magical items, mysterious places, and dangerous battles. Your hero will have to grow in order to reach the goal of the game, but don’t worry, the hero will grow as the world around them is explored.

This game is also a story. The major plot of And Away is always the same, but what you do during the game and if you survive is up to you.

It can be played an endless amount of times, without ever experiencing the same game twice.

And Away consists of one 41 card deck, which represents the first act of the story. Players will start with all the cards FaceDown in a pile, and two marked “starting cards” flipped FaceUp in a second pile.

Progression through the game occurs by Flipping FaceDown cards and either Acknowledging (bringing them to the FaceUp pile) or Ignoring (Cycling them back under the FaceDown pile) based on the game state until each card in the Deck is FaceUp.

boardgame: Sentinels of the Multiverse: Kvothe Six-String Argent Adept Promo Card
description:
This official Greater than Games promo card was announced by author Patrick Rothfuss on his blog in November 2014. The character Kvothe is originally from Rothfuss' Kingkiller chronicles series of books.

All sale proceeds are donated to Heifer International, a charity that helps people raise themselves up out of poverty and starvation by promoting education, sustainable agriculture, and local industry all over the world.

From the Official Website

What ho! Kvothe Six-String﻿ enters the multiverse as an alternate hero to play as with the Argent Adept hero deck (contained in the expansion) based on the character from Patrick Rothfuss's Kingkiller Chronicle. Kvothe Six-String has different powers and abilities than the Argent Adept to add a new twist to the game.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
Players use custom dice as pawns to move around a modular hexagonal board with 12 action spaces. When landing on a particular space, the player may acquire an action card from that space. If all cards have been taken, the player may capture the piece of the board itself, leaving fewer spaces for the other players to traverse.

When a player moves through a space that already has an opponent's pawn, the opponent launches an ambush. If the opponent has a higher power level (indicated on the face of the die), the opponent may steal a victory point. However, there are also opportunities for the active player to attack as well. Action cards can be used to alter the face of the die, changing the movement points and power level of the player's pawn.

The game ends at the end of the round in which either all "soul shard" cards (acquired by making a circuit of the board) or "destiny markers" (victory point tokens) are gone from the supply. The player(s) with the most victory points wins.

This abstract game uses pieces shaped like macaron cookies in five different colors (pink, blue, brown, green, and yellow) with four different colors of filling (pink, brown, green, and white). The pieces are played on a cloth board with a checkered grid playing area.

The game comes with two sets of rules. For rule set #1 (2 players only), players begin with 7 pink, 5 brown, and 5 green pieces each and take turns placing them on the board. The winner is the first person to line up at least three pieces of the same color in a row. If both players place all of their pieces without getting three in a row, the second player wins.

For rule set #2 (2-4 players), players take turns drawing random pieces from a bag and placing them on the board. If a player manages to surround any number of pieces in a line (horizontal, vertical, or diagonal) with two cookies of the same color (one on either end), the player captures all of the surrounded pieces. The captured pieces are worth points at the end of the game. Bonus points are obtained by capturing sets of certain color combinations. When all pieces have been played, the person with the most points wins.

Typical roll-and-move game on the theme "airplane racing". Roll one die, and at landing on certain fields the player must for example move back or forwards or sit one round out. First to land on field #60 is the winner. If you bet money on the planes, the winner get 2/3 of the cash pool; second get the rest.
The game is in Norwegian, probably printed in the late 1920s or early 1930s.

This is the Norwegian edition of Moderner Wettkampf.

Typical roll-and-move game on the theme "modern racing". You must chose to be either a runner, an automobile driver or an airplane pilot. Roll one die, and at landing on certain fields the player must for example move back or forwards or sit one round out. First to land on field #40 is the winner. If you bet money, the winner get 2/3 of the cash pool; second get the rest.
The game is in Norwegian, probably printed in the late 1920s or early 1930s.

Typical roll-and-move game on the theme "Association football". Roll one die, and at landing on certain fields the player must for example move backwards or forwards or sit one round out. First to land on field #50 (#65 in the german version) is the winner.
The game is in Norwegian/Danish, probably printed in the late 1920s. The title is misspelled "Fottball" instead of "Fotball". Also, the label "Amuso" is printed on the box cover. The german version is called "Fussball".

Players are dealt cards depicting girls, ghosts, or fairies. On each turn, one player selects an opponent and plays a card face down to "visit" that opponent for Halloween, saying "Trick or Treat." The opponent must decide whether to hand over a piece of candy or suffer a "trick."

The results of the "trick" are different depending on the identity of the card. If the card is a girl, the opponent receives two pieces of candy. If the card is a ghost, the opponent must give two pieces of candy to the card's owner. If the card is a fairy, the opponent and the card's owner both must give up half their candy to the supply.

If the opponent elects the "treat" option, the active player may request a second piece of candy...but if so, the opponent may choose to turn the played card back on its owner. The game ends when all cards have been played. The player(s) with the most candy wins.

boardgame: Hell Hath No Fury: Tank-to-Tank Combat
description:
From the Publisher's website:

What: Tank specific wargame/RPG rules for World War II.

Scale: Tank on tank combat. 1 model/figure equals 1 vehicle.

Your Role: Players can command from one tank up to a company of tanks.

Playability: Designed for solo, same side and head to head play.

Hell Hath No Fury can be played in a variety of ways:

    You can play as a Tank Commander with one tank.
    You can play as a Platoon Leader with a platoon of 3 - 5 tanks. 
    You can play as a Company Commander with 2 or more platoons.
    You can play one-off battles.
    You can play a full campaign with up to ten scenarios.


The bottom line is you can play it any way you like, with any models or counters you like.

Inside you’ll find:

    Rules for tank to tank combat with stats and counters for a variety of American and German vehicles
    Ten scenarios covering the period from D-Day to the fall of Berlin.
    Fully compatible with NUTS, our WW2 game.
    A minimal bookkeeping Campaign System that gives meaning to every game as the results of one will affect the outcome of the next.

boardgame: The Global Diversity Game for International Relief and Development Organizations
description:
From the rulebook:
The Global Diversity Game for International Relief and Development Organizations is a highly interactive training tool using a multiple-choice quiz format to explore facts about global diversity.

Game play:
The objective of the game is to be the first to reach the center square of the board, and answer a question correctly to win.  Players start on the "Go" square and roll dice to move around the track. On each space where they land, they must answer a question from one of six decks, all of which focus on some aspect of global diversity. A correct answer earns the player a colored chip.  Tow chips of any one color may be traded for a single chip of any other color. When they have all outer track chips, players may move into the inner track. The first player to reach the center space and answer a question chosen by the team to their left wins.

Extraordinary is a superhero-themed strategy card game by designer Roger Alan Cotton for 2 players, ages 12 and up.

PREMISE

Players each control a team of seven super-powered characters who battle through an episode from an original comic book issue story line.

Each Episode is comprised of seven Hero Objectives and seven competing Villain Objectives.

In order to complete an objective, a hero or villain must use a requisite super power (listed on the Objective Card) to perform a specific Feat (listed next to the super power on the Objective Card).

To complete the Feat, a character must roll a result that equals or exceeds the Objective's target Completion Point Value (CPV).

Should the character successfully perform the Objective Feat, an opponent's character may still attempt to thwart it. This is accomplished in the manner explained above.

If the Thwart Feat roll result equals or exceeds its target CPV, it is successful, and the opponent's Objective Feat fails. In addition, the thwarting team gains a two-point advantage on its next turn, when attempting to complete its own objective.

Either team may attempt to complete their unfinished objectives on their subsequent turns.

However, as soon as either side completes an objective that isn't thwarted, that part of the Episode story line is "locked down," and the Objective Card is "won" by the team that completed it.

______________________________________________________________________________________

OBJECTIVE

Win four or more of the Episode's seven objectives.

______________________________________________________________________________________


GAME SET UP

1. Decide who will play the Heroes League and who will play the Villains League. Place the appropriate Player Mat on the playing area in front of you.

2. Decide which Comic Book Issue you will be playing. Bring out this issue’s corresponding Issue Cover Card and place it off to the side of the play area, between the players for reference. Next to this card, place that Issue’s Episode Card, and the corresponding Episode Objective Cards (stacked face-down and in sequence).

3. Each Player selects (7) super heroes or super villains and places them face-down on the playing area before them. These characters should have power icons and keywords on them that match as many power icons and keywords listed on the Episode Objective Cards.

Shuffle each of these characters’ (5) Action Cards in with the (25) core cards in your League Deck.

4. Take all of the Resource Cards and separate them into four face-down card stacks: NPCs, Powers, Tech, and Upgrades. Shuffle each card stack and flip face-up the top card of each stack.

5. Determine First Player

Each player rolls (1) die. The player who rolls the highest result is the Active Player during Turn 1. Reroll any tied roll results.

From Turn 2 onward, players alternate being the Active Player.

6. Both players flip their characters face-up.

7. Draw (7) cards each to form your starting Hands.

______________________________________________________________________________________

GAME TURN PHASES

Each game turn in XO! is divided into five phases:

1. Status Check Phase
2. Card Discard/Draw Phase
3. Attack Phase
4. Feat/Thwart Phase
5. Turn Resolution Phase

______

1. STATUS CHECK Phase

(This phase is skipped during the first turn)

Remove Status cards tagged to any Characters or Objects in play who were <Blinded>, <Short-Circuited>, <Staggered>, <Stunned>, or <Winded>.

Characters or Objects in play who are tagged with the <Damaged>, <Injured>, <Incapacitated>, <Prone>, <Sabotaged>, or <Trapped> Status Cards retain their Status cards until other cards are played that instruct players to remove them.

2. CARD DISCARD / DRAW Phase

On the First Turn, both players draw (7) cards to form their Hands.

At the start of every turn thereafter, players may each discard up to (2) cards from their Hands, and then draw back up to (7) cards.

3. ATTACK Phase

During this phase, the Active Player may attack with as many characters as he wishes (as long as those characters are able to do so, and have the requisite Energy Cards to use cards from the Active Player’s Hand).

NOTE: Card play may change the order of some attacks, regardless of who the Active Player is at the time.

- Combat Rounds

Each Combat Round is divided into three segments: Hit Determination segment, Damage Effect Determination segment, and Counterattack segment.

Each attack is resolved in whatever order the Active Player desires. The Responding Player may play applicable defenses and modifier cards to counter the attacks.

The Active Player indicates which character in play is attacking, and who the defending enemy character or target will be.

Hit Determination Segment

During this phase segment, you determine if an attack hits or misses.

The Attacking Character (Attacker) rolls the number of dice indicated by the value on his or her card’s Attack Icon. The Defending Character (Defender) rolls the number of dice indicated by the value on his or her card’s Defense Icon.
The Attacker’s attack roll result (plus any applicable modifiers) becomes his/her Attack Combat Value (ACV). The Defender’s attack roll result (plus any applicable modifiers) becomes his/her Defense Combat Value (DCV).
Compare the DCV to the ACV:
If the DCV equals or exceeds the ACV, the attack fails, and the Defender takes no damage, and suffers no attack effects. The Attack Phase immediately ends.

If the ACV exceeds the DCV, the attack hits, and the Defender takes damage. Proceed to the Damage Effect Determination segment.

Damage Effect Determination Segment

During this phase segment, you determine how much damage the Defender sustains, and what effect, if any, the attack has on the Defender’s Readiness Status.

The difference between the Attacker’s ACV and the Defender’s DCV is the amount of damage the Defender sustains. This Damage Value (plus any Attack Effect Damage) is compared to the Defender’s Health Value which is listed within his/her card’s Health Icon:

If the amount of damage sustained is less than the Defender’s Health Value, the character remains in play. If the attack has a listed effect, that attack effect is now applied (see Attack Effects).
If the amount of damage sustained is equal to the Defender’s Health Value, the character remains in play but is tagged with the <Stunned> Status card.
If the amount of damage sustained by the Defender is greater than his/her Health Value, the Defender is <KOd> and placed on top of the League Deck discard pile.

- Attack Effects

Different attacks have different effects. For <Blinded>, <Burned>, <Frozen>, <Horrified>, <Knocked Back>, <Staggered>, or <Stunned> attack effects, tag the Defender’s card with the appropriate Status card.

Tagging a card means to attach a Status Card to it.

Follow the instructions on attached Status Cards to determine their impact on characters and objects for the rest of the turn.

- Moffed Attacks and Defenses

If a Defender or Attacker rolls (1) or more dice, and all of the roll results equal (0), that defense or attack is considered to have “monumentally failed” (moffed).

When the Defender’s DCV is moffed, s/he sustains (+1) Die of Damage during the Damage Effect Determination segment.

When the Attacker’s ACV is moffed, the Defender has the opportunity to immediately counterattack.

Counterattack Segment

A Defender may only counterattack if s/he has the Counterattack ability, or if the Attacker moffed his/her ACV.

To resolve the Counterattack, follow the standard combat resolution steps listed above. The Counterattack’s Damage Type is determined by the Defender’s Superpower Icon (listed on the Character card’s upper-left corner).

Once all of the Active Player’s attacks have been resolved, his Attacks Phase ends.

- AOE Damage Effects

Some attacks indicate they are AOE Attacks. This means they are Area of Affect attacks which affect both the defending target of the attack and any characters adjacent to the Defender.

5. FEATS COMPLETION Phase

During this phase, the Active Player assigns a ready character in play with the requisite Power icon (or Keyword) to attempt a feat that will complete his league’s current Episode Objective.

- Resolving Feat Attempt

The Active Player rolls as many dice as s/he can to attempt the feat (based on the numbers indicated on the acting character’s card, as well as on any applicable cards from the Active Player’s Hand):

If the Feat Attempt roll result (plus any modifiers) equals or exceeds the Feat Completion Value (FCV) indicated within the Completion diamond, the feat is successfully completed.
Otherwise, the feat attempt fails, and the phase ends.
Should the Active Player’s feat attempt succeed, the Responding Player may assign a ready character in play to attempt to thwart the feat.

To do this, the Responding Player’s character must have the requisite Power icon or Keyword to attempt the thwart:

If the Thwart Attempt roll result (plus any modifiers) equals or exceeds the Thwart Completion Value (TCV) indicated within the Completion diamond, the feat is successfully completed.
In this case, the Responding Player gains a 2-point bonus on his or her next turn, when attempting to complete his or her League’s Episode Objective.
If the thwart attempt fails, the Active Player “wins” the Episode Objective Card. The card is removed from the Story Line, and placed in the Active Player’s Victory Area.
SCORING

Once all seven Episode Objective Cards have been completed, the player with four or more of them wins the game.

6. TURN RESOLUTION Phase

Once the Active Player has finished attacking and attempting a feat, his turn is over. If all seven Objective Cards are completed, the game ends, and you proceed to Scoring.

Otherwise, the next player becomes the Active Player, and the game proceeds.
 ______________________________________________________________________________________

COMBAT DICE

Each player gets (7) six-sided dice to use during the game. The Heroes Player uses the White Mask dice and the Villains Player uses the Black Mask dice.

Each combat die has six sides with different values listed on them. One side has the (0) value, two sides have the (1) value, two sides have the (2) value, and the last side has the (3) value.

May 10th, 1864. A solid month of fighting found Grant no closer to the destruction of Lee's army than when his offensive started. Indeed, his losses exceeded Lee's by nearly two thirds. After escaping disaster in the Wilderness (when arguably only the wounding of Confederate General James Longstreet salvaged a stalemate from sure defeat), Grant now found himself grinding his army against the deadly abrasive of Rebel entrenchments.

Desperate for a clean, quick solution, Grant listened attentively to the notions of a brash 24 year old Colonel named Emory Upton. Attack on a narrow front, Upton maintained, and do not pause to fire. The condensed mass, several lines deep, would simply ram its way across the parapets. The first line, after making initial penetration would then fan left and right, widening the breach. The succeeding lines would drive straight ahead, deepening the plunge. A reserve would then arrive, to stem the sure gray counterattack.

Mule Shoe Salient is a regimental-level simulation of the Federal assault on May 12, 1864 at The Bloody Angle, which was the culmination of the Battle of Spotsylvania.

Each unit represents several companies of 120 men/strength point, or artillery sections representing 2 guns. Players alternate moving units and firing. The Federal player wins if their units occupy three or more victory hexes, while the Confederate player wins if no victory hexes are occupied by Federal troops. Any other result is a draw.

11x17 map, 140 die-cut counters, 12 page rulebook.

(source: One Small Step website and user's description)

A light political game involving vote placement and based on "The 2010 Election" but aiming to cover the May 2015 election. It attempts to model the movement into four party politics and the rise of the SNP.

The two players (Labour and Conservatives) place population markers derived from opinion polls (run by Press Cards) in 32 representative Constituencies. The Constituencies are grouped by type (rural, industrial, suburban) and by the three home nations (England, Scotland and Wales - Northern Ireland is dealt with separately). Other parties are placed according to rules that mimic their strengths and weaknesses by random draw from the Scrabble bag.

In each phase of the game different placing rules apply as you move from placing your hardcore supporters (who tend to be in safe areas) up into the floating voters (who can be anywhere) on Election Night.

At last we abandon further fiddling and offer (for a mere sum of £5 via paypal; to charles.vasey@gmail.com) the next in the series from the 2010 Election. This time we are trying it in advance of the actual event.

The game kit (for it is a mere primitive PNP) is a number of pdfs and spreadies giving you the map of UK represented as blocks of constituencies, the counters, and the press cards. It is pretty much an experience game.

There will be an update after the actual Election.

The pdfs should resize for Letter printing, but if not I'll save in Letter and then copy over.

What can we do with just 6 numbers—0, 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5? Anything and everything! That’s the beauty and mystery of numbers. And this amazing possibility is beautifully captured in Finger Bomb™!

What makes Finger Bomb™ truly amazing is that it has taken just these 6 numbers and created an amazing array of 50 things we can do with them:

10 FUNtastic games
15 hands-on lessons
13 investigative activities
12 brain-boosting puzzles

Finger Bomb™ is a card deck system. It is part of a package that includes a card deck and a companion book. It is primarily designed for use in learning mathematics.  It is most suitable for kids aged 5 - 8.

This basketball game starts like the real thing, with a "jump ball" to determine starting player.  From your hand and the two decks, two players go head to head placing players on their basketball team and defining the offense and defense.  First draw from the player deck and play a card, then draw from the coaches deck and play a card each turn.  Player cards will only score for you if they are played in the right positions on the court.  This results in free-throws, jump-shots or 3 pointers. The coaches deck helps you pick which offense or defense both teams are using. Use the REF card to remove your opponents key player just before the quarter ends. With this simple 2 deck card game, you can re-create four quarters of a basketball game.

The Advanced Tobruk System returns to Finland in this module pitting the might and mass of the Soviet war machine against the resourceful and determined Finns during The Winter War of 1939.

This module comes with four beautifully-rendered geomorphic maps, six scenarios, all the personnel, weapon and vehicle counters required to play (including new artwork for the Finns) and a Battlefield Walkaround booklet with special rules and historical notes.

Standard ATS markers, playaids and Rules are required and not included.

boardgame: Siege of Love: The Lady Matsu Hime Razan Summer Affair
description:
THE TIME IS: 1200 hours, 3 June 1615. The Shogun finally senses the approach of the end of a long term conflict. Toyotomi house still does not submit to the Shogun’s will.

Prologue
Father Ieyasu had it all thought out - clean up things at Osaka quickly to have a summer saké party with luscious Lady Matsu hime Razan at 1800 hours. The plan is set. Ieyasu had also interests in martial arts, tea, and foreign intercourse.

Osaka Battlefield 1000 hours.
Ieyasu: Ohhhh, Lady Razan!
Lady Razan: Oh! Tokugawa-sama!
Ieyasu: You’ve managed to come to the battlefield! May I know why?
Lady Razan: Oh! Yes, Oh! You naughty naughty Tokugawa! I’m your living reminder of our summer saké party …Oh! You’re even worse than Hidetada…
Ieyasu: Please leave at once! This is a dangerous place for a delicatessen as you. And don’t worry… I’ve instructed the Generals in our war council. I’ve explained them the notions of priorities and timings…
Lady Razan: OK! mumbo-jumbo-sama! 1800 hours, not a minute more… You have been warned!
Old man that was passing by chance, whispering, through the battlefield: And so it was. Everyone was at bliss, at least at Tokugawa’s mighty army. The low rank soldiers were partying since 0600 hours. Generals were laughing, confident and very moody about the upcoming battle. Ieyasu’s son, the Shogun Tokugawa Hidetada, began to move his full army in four parallel lines, prepared to make flanking maneuvers of their own. Oh yes I know… Richard Wilhelm already told that “The way to study the past is not to confine oneself to mere knowledge of history but, through application of this knowledge, to give actuality to the past”.

Osaka Main Castle 1130 hours.
A Toyotomi ninja reported Ieyasu’s plan. He was aware that Lady Razan would be somewhere in the battlefield… If somehow they could resist until 1800 hours… a last samurai plot was in forge…
Ninja: Grand Massster Toyotomi-sssama I wasss near master’sss SSSanada army and I wasss able to sssee it with my own armsss, hear with my own hair and sssmelling with my own fingersss ninja.
Hideyori: I knew it! That cheap sl*t! And are you aware of why I knew?
Ninja: sss no Massster Toyotomi-sssama ninja.
Hideyori: A long time ago, around two solar calendar years, I had a close relation with Lady Razan based on a conspiring plot against the old man Ieyasu, Shogun at that time… But that was before I decked in love for her…
The in meanwhile old man was passing through Osaka castle, whispering: Toyotomi immediately issued a hammer-and-anvil operation, in which his strongest core would attack the center of the Tokugawa Army, while a second force, would flank them from the rear to scare Lady Razan out of the battlefield. History tells that “…legendary swordsman Miyamoto Musashi participated in the battle on the Tokugawa Army side, though there is no recorded documental base in this battle…”.

THE TIME IS: 1200 hours, 3 June 1615. 
A 0001 hour comical and competitive 2 player Super Postcard Game.

Simon Swipe the Card Game for when you feel like defeating your friends with cards instead of the game. Be the first player to get rid of your cards each round and have the lowest score at the end of the game.

The game is played in rounds. A round ends when one player has played all his cards. You then tally your points and the player to the left shuffles and deals a new hand.

Each player begins with 9 cards, the the top card of the draw deck is flipped over, if its a wild card keep flipping. This determines the first card of the pattern.

On your turn place a matching color, swipe or wild card on top of the first card.If you cannot play a card you must keep drawing till you do. Then place another card next to the first one, keep doing this until you run out of cards. You can start a pattern even if you can't finish it. If you finish it and have a card left over, add it to the pattern.

When one person runs out of cards, its time to tally points. Any cards left in your hand are equal to 1 point. when 1 person gets to 15 points then the game is over. If no one gets 15 points that hand, then shuffle the cards and play another hand. If there is a tie all tied players win.

Expansion kit for Planes: High Pilotage game.

Kit includes 1 Bravo model (which you assemble snapfit) and 1 Bravo Plane card for the game.

There is no additional flight stand included.

Expansion kit for Planes: High Pilotage game.

Kit includes 1 El Chupacabra model (which you assemble snapfit) and 1 El Chupacabra  Plane card for the game.

There is no additional flight stand included.

Expansion kit for Planes: High Pilotage game.

Kit includes 1 Rochelle model (which you assemble snapfit) and 1 Rochelle Plane card for the game.

There is no additional flight stand included.

Expansion kit for Planes: High Pilotage game.

Kit includes 1 Skipper Riley model (which you assemble snapfit) and 1 Skipper Riley Plane card for the game.

There is no additional flight stand included.

This is a two player game played with a skeletonal 5 by 5 unit cube.

Each player starts with eight 2 by 1 pieces, either black or white, which can be neatly but firmly slid inside the holes in the cube in any of the three dimensions.

The object of the game is to be the first player to connect any two opposite faces of the cube with pieces of their colour such that each link is at least a 1x1 area.

Players take it in turn adding a piece and if no one completes a chain after all 16 pieces have been added, the game is a draw.

boardgame: Les Maréchaux III: Augereau 1814 et Eugene 1814
description:
The Marshals III covers the campaigns of winter 1814 that took place in France north of Lyon and in Italy on the Mincio. These theatres of operation are considered to be minor since they occurred far from the Emperor’s view who was engaged in the north of France. Nevertheless, important and little-known battles took place there where the young Marie-Louise, the conscripts of the French army, displayed extraordinary courage. The first campaign, that of Marshal Augereau in France, recounts in three scenarios the march on the flanks of the Austrian army engaged against Napoleon.

In the first, the “Lion of Castiglione” must seize Geneva to cover his march on the enemy lines of communication. The second relates the Austrians’ offensive return from Dijon and the fall of Lyon after the battle of Limonest. The third covers the whole campaign from the beginnings of Augereau’s timid offensive to the loss of the “capital of the Gauls”. The second campaign, led by Eugène de Beauharnais, describes the fierce combats for the supremacy of northern Italy. Threatened at his rear by the betrayal of Murat who is moving up from the south of the “boot” with his Neapolitans, Eugène withdrew from Verona to Mantua. The first scenario, very brief, simulates the about-turn of the French on the Mincio to counter the Austrian advance. The second covers the whole campaign from the evacuation of Verona to the victories of Eugène against various enemy threats.

With this new volume, The Marshals series evolves. The more imposing strengths of these campaigns required some adaptations that allowed for simulating the march of an entire army corps. These new rules are compatible with the previous modules with some minor adaptations.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet
family: Players: Wargames with Rules Supporting Only Two Players

description:
Tomodachi Maze is a small, solitaire, maze game based on the Japanese Momotaro folk tale. Two double-sided cards are included as the play surface. Players start at the center of of the start card and move around the paths until they reach the edge of the card. At that point, you can do one of two things:

1) Flip the card
2) Explore

If you do either of these things and the path does not connect, you cannot continue this way and you must go back and try a new path. Continue until you find each of Momotaro's friends and then head off to Ghost Island to finish!

In Dirty Little Secrets players are detectives with a slightly loose code of ethics. You’ve used some tactics you’re not proud of, but it’s always gotten the job done. Now some of the other detectives are on a case that might reveal your dirty little secret. Can’t let that happen, so better you expose their secret first. At least your partner has your back; seems it’s hard to find someone you can rely on in this department.

Players will work with their partner to use deduction and rough tactics to uncover your opponents’ cards, while using misdirection to cover your own tracks. Players must gather evidence, information about opponents’ cards, and choose the right times to make accusations. Be careful about it, though, as every action you take has a trade-off, and often this gives your opponents knowledge to use against you. 



				
				
					Aim of the GameYou (and your partner) are trying to have the most Points at the end of the game. Points are gained by keeping your most valuable card secret until the end of the game, and using collected evidence to remove your opponents’ cards. During the game you’ll use your chosen Action cards to gain information about opponents’ cards, and tactically position your cards to confound your opponents and open up new Actions for you to use. The game ends immediately after all the Evidence is collected.







				
				
					Gameplay OverviewEach player has the same 8 Action cards. They are each numbered with the Time – 1 a.m. up to 8 a.m. This Time doubles as the card’s value in points if it scores at the end of the game. Players will choose 5 cards to play with each game, keeping their choices secret. 
The game begins with a shared Evidence pool and every player with five face-down hidden cards: three forming a Front Line row and two making a Back Office row. In a 3- or 4-player game, there is also a Chief’s Badge in the centre of play that is given to a player whenever they lose a card.

In turn, players take up to 2 unique actions:


     Collect an Evidence
     Accuse an opponent’s card
     Use a particular card from your Front Line (Players can use 2 different cards on their turn, just not the same one twice).


Or they can spend BOTH actions to:

     Cover their tracks (rearrange 3 of their cards and flip them upside down again)
     Claim the Chief’s badge from another player, or use the Chief’s badge as any card action (In 3- 4-player games).


Early in the game, players will primarily collect Evidence and use their Front Line cards to gain information. Later, they’ll manipulate setups and try to use Evidence to eliminate cards from play. 



Evidence

Collecting Evidence is crucial to victory, because you need it to make accusations. By itself, Evidence is worth 1 point for every 2 you have. But if you successfully use one to accuse an opponent, it goes into your Evidence Locker and is worth a full point. If you accuse incorrectly, however, the Evidence goes to your opponent.  



Accusations
To win you’ll need to reduce your opponents’ score by eliminating their high value cards. You can make random guesses, but that wastes both valuable Evidence and actions. Instead, you’ll want to spend actions using your cards to get information first. Then just before they cover their tracks to remove what you know, you make an accusation. If you’re right the Evidence doubles in value to a full point. But if you’re wrong, it’s lost to your opponent. Or worse, if you mis-accuse their Red Herring card, they get an additional Evidence from the pool.



Using a card

You can use any card that’s still hidden in your Front line – but be careful! Don’t point at it, flip it, or do anything that might indicate WHICH card you’re using. Unless, that is, you’re trying to mislead your opponent.

The low value/Time cards are powerful actions, as they will get you solid information like the Time difference between 2 cards of an opponent, or which cards in their Front line have “finger-prints” on them. This will help you deduce their cards, while other actions like the Frame-up, provide you with tactical moves to reposition some of your – or an opponent’s – cards. 



Bluffing?

In the basic game you are only able to take the actions of cards you legitimately have in your Front Line. This provides a more deduction and tactical game. However, optional bluffing rules allow players to take any action they wish – legal or not. But be careful that your opponent doesn’t call you out that you’re lying, or you’ll start losing cards! 



Covering your tracks

As your valuable cards and actions are revealed by opponents, or as they gain more information, you’ll want to get a little breathing room. Covering your tracks may take your whole turn but it lets you choose 3 of your cards (Front line or in your reserved Back Office) to hide again and place wherever you like. It’s a great way to get back in the game, or foil an opponent before they have the chance to accuse you.



The Chief’s badge
The Chief would prefer that her detectives are focused on other criminal matters, and so in 3- and 4-player games, whenever a player loses a card to an accusation they receive the Chief’s badge for protection. A player with the Chief’s badge cannot be accused, and can use both their actions to activate the badge as any card – in play or not.








				
				
					End of the GameOnce the last Evidence token is claimed, the game ends. It’s a timer, as once collected, Evidence never re-enters the central pool. 
Players score most of their points from the highest card they managed to keep hidden in their Front Line at the end of the game. Note that the Dirty Secret card is valued at 4 a.m., but if IT is the scoring card (highest one hidden), it scores an extra 7 points. The trick, of course, is making sure it’s both hidden AND the highest Time in the front at game-end.

Each Players also score points for their collected Evidence


     1 per 2 unused Evidence
     1 per Evidence in locker (used to successfully accuse)









Summary

Dirty Little Secrets is a game of ‘playing the player’ as much as it is a game about your card-play. Good players are highly responsive to their opponents, and learn to push and prod player behaviours to their favour. With only 8 cards, it’s not about having the right card – it’s about knowing when and how to use it.

For rookies, there is a suggested set of cards to use that have more straight-forward actions that let these players be effective until they learn some of their own tricks and strategies.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
Year 2100, world industry has focused on war machines called IRONS and countries compete to have the best Irons teams. Laboratories
look for the best weapons and clubs look for the best pilots.
Countries fight in the famous and hardest league in the world:IRON LEAGUE.
And there are hundreds of teams that fight in each country to compete in the Iron League.
You are an Iron team coach and you have been hired to take the team to victory and win the Iron League title.
Management and sponsors place their trust in you; we know you are the best and you will take the team to victory.
The decisions related to the selection of your team and of your cards, together with the luck of the dice and of the events cards
will be decisive to be able to win or lose. Score more times with the plasma ball than the opposing team;
you can also damage all the rival Irons or hit them with the plasma ball to win points.

The game contains: 3 boards, 6 complete Irons with 2 “silent” rotating machine-guns, 2 Zim guns, 2 shields, 2 beams and 2 light machine-guns (The arms, legs and weapons can be joined together by hand), 1 goal square, 62 cards (24 events cards and 38 action cards), 2 action dices, 1 plasma ball counter, 2 scoreboard counters, 8 damage counters, 6 Iron sheets, 1 summary sheet with scoreboard and 6 pilot identifiers.

Main features:
-Giant robots which measure more than 9 cm, with a lot of details and made of a very resistant ABS plastic.
-Interchangeable legs, arms and weapons. The robots get the weapons from the battle field.
-Random event cards. They can be good, bad or nothing may occur.
-Action cards, which help and provide you with improvements during the game. 
-Only one goal place. You can quickly change from attack to defense, be careful!
-Players: 2-6

Action dice:
The action dice are used to carry out actions such as shooting,launching the plasma ball at a target, stealing the ball, etc…
2 action dice are always thrown. There are 5 icons on the dice:
2 pilots: When you throw a dice and you get this drawing, it means 2 points.
1 pilot: When you throw a dice and you get this drawing, it means 1 point.
1 pilot: When you throw a dice and you get this drawing, it means 1 point.
Faulty engine: When you throw a dice and you get this drawing, it means -1 point.
Example of throw: The result is: 2 pilots+1 pilot= +2 +1 =3 points.
Plasma ball: Only used to steal plasma ball.

You need 1 Iron sheet for each Iron of your team. The damagepoints of your Iron are indicated on it and it is associated with a pilot card.
All the weapons that come with the game must be placed on the main board.
Game sequence
1- Pick up 1 Events card
2- Pick up 1 Action card.
3- Movement phase, shoot and plasma ball.
4- Damage
5- Scoreboard.
During your turn each Iron robot has 2 actions.
You can choose these actions:
Move 1 or 2 squares= 1 action
Shoot one time= 1 action
Attack close up= 1 action (Attack with beam, charge/push or slap)
Get up=1 action
Push=1 action
Pass the ball=1 action
Receive the ball=1 action
Trying to hit an enemy with the plasma ball=1 action
Trying to steal the plasma ball=1 action
Examples:
Example 1: Move 1 or 2 squares and try to pass the ball to a companion.

You cannot throw the ball at the goal square. When an Iron robot from your team gets the plasma ball into the goal area, you get 8 points, when you destroy 1 enemy Iron robot, you get 1 point,when you hit it with the plasma ball, you get 2 points.
Use the counters with the “Iron League” logo and use them on the scoreboard to remember the points won.
The turn following a Goal begins with the team that has received the Goal starting the next phase, and the team that scored. All the Irons are automatically repaired and changed places.Each team at one end.

Take your Iron robots to victory!

Roll a’ Lantern is a real time dice rolling affair where players rapidly roll their dice to trying to match the faces of the die to the Jack o’ Lantern card in play. When a player matches the dice faces to the current card in play, they yell out Jack o' Lantern and claim that card. The player with the most Jack o' Lantern cards claimed when the deck runs out is declared the winner.

Logo: What Am I? is a "no-questions" version of The Logo Board Game. Players describe, draw and guess the brands and products shown on the cards. The first player or team to reach the centre of the board wins.

This edition is designed to be a little more child friendly and as such has a lower suggested age.

Logo Grab is a very different game from the usual brand heavy trivia associated with The Logo Board Game Family.

Instead players are competing to pick up logo cards from a grid which match the dice rolled. Players are collecting sets of cards. The dice grabbing adds an element of speed and dexterity, but players must beware, some of the dice contain bells and if you grab a bell die you do not get to keep the card you just claimed!

A Dice 10,000-like game using Backgammon doubling cubes.
A simple solution is included for those who do not have any doubling cubes.
The same general rules and ideas apply, but scoring has been considerably changed by the fact of using doubling cubes instead of dice.
The individual digits of a number on the face of the cubes come into play.
Reimplements "Dice 10,000" but adds some twists only possible with doubling cubes.

Pounce is a fast paced dexterity game for 2-8 players that can be played as either individuals or in teams. Throughout the game small cups move around the table and players attempt to bounce a ball into the cup in front of them. If the player on your right completes this task before you, they can stack their cup on top of yours. The stacked player has to take a tower penalty shot – potentially losing one of their lives and suffering a temporary handicap. The game ends when only one player or team has lives remaining.

This two-game set includes Edward de Bono's L-Game and 3-Spot. Both are games of pure skill, designed to improve thinking skills. They can be as challenging as chess, yet as easy as playing tic-tac-toe and can be learned in minutes.

In L-Game the objective is to move your L-shaped piece and one of 2 neutral pieces to block the other player's pieces. There are 16 (4x4) squares on the board with 18,368 possible moves.

In 3-Spot the objective is to move your piece and the neutral piece to cover the spots on a nine (3x3) square board. The unique feature is: a player can, at any time, change strategies from an offensive tactic to a defensive one, or vise versa, and WIN!

Plan, Play and Goal!

Description
Goal: the Match is a thrilling Collectible Football Card Game that simulates a soccer match using an strategic view between two players, from now on  they’re gonna be called Managers to distinguish them from football players.

You could play a complete game of modern football: Goals, fouls, yellow/red cards, set pieces, tackles, waste of time, etc. in a game of 40 minutes approximately.

In Goal: the Match you could assemble a team, the cards that will lead your style and the action cards that your team will deploy. All those cards will be get through a basic box and random card packs.

Football players allows you to play cards, style cards will mark the general path about how they will be played and the action deck will determine what and how the team plays the match.

A brief  summary

At the beginning of the turn. The manager with initiative will choose to realize one of the following turns: Move Turn or Highlight Turn.

1) Move turn: Represents the major and boring part of the match and normally doesn’t appear in the highlights of the news. In this turn teams deploy their game. It’s divided in two phases: Initiative and Replica.

In this type of turn, the manager will start the Initiative phase by playing cards from hand (cost of cards is paid by field players), activating player special abilities and/or interacting with strategy cards previously deployed and style cards(They are deployed from the beginning). What is more, the cost in minutes of every played card will be increase the total minutes of the match. The Initiative phase ends when he stops playing cards or abilities.

After that the opponent Manager will play a replica phase, in which he could carry a similar phase up to the maximum of minutes played in the Initiative phase by the opponent. Taking into account that some of the cards can only be played in the initiative phase(marked with an icon in the cost). This phase means the opponent is playing and deploying its strategy, not just waiting looking at the match.

2) Highlight turn: It represents what is amazing in a match , those scenes we watch in the TV match summaries. Contains up to two phases, attack phase and a possible counterattack phase(or even more than one).

Phase attack begins with the manager with initiative increasing the time in one minute. Beginning from him the following possibilities are gonna be played. a) Fast action card from hand b) Abilities from strategy card previously deployed c) Player abilities. When no one wants to continue, Both will  throw 3 dices of 6 faces. Also the defending manager will throw two incidence dices (they signal possible fouls,yellow/red cards, injuries, set pieces, etc) the difference after bonuses will mark what has happened: Goal, Dangerous play, good defence and [b]CounterAttack[/b].

At the end of replica turn or Highlight turn both Managers untap players and cards in the field and draw two more cards 2 (with a hand limit of 8 cards).

QR Code
Each one of the game cards has a qr code QR that redirect to our database directly to this card page where any manager could solve doubts and everything about that card.

Manager ID
Manager ID (MID) cames along with each copy of Goal: The Match. It’s used to measure your level as a manager. After being registered you may annotate results of your matches with other Managers with MID in order to follow statistics. At the end of season you may win amazing prizes!!

Space Perimeter is a game where companies (players) explore space by playing square cards in their hand to form the playing board. Cards will have a numerical value on each side. Once a card has been placed, players will add up the total value on the side of the map facing them. This value, or Acclaim, will fluctuate during the course of the game.

Players can use Acclaim to build Space Stations, deploy Spy Probes, or colonize Planets. The goal of the game is to have the highest Acclaim after the last card is drawn.

From the publisher's website:

Combat Leader: East Front is the foundational game of the series, and contains all game components and standard rules (plus advanced options) necessary to play.  Players command a single German or Russian squad in rural (and varied) terrain during the initial months of Operation Barbarossa in 1941.  Besides the supplied scenarios, there is an almost limitless number of situations you can design on your own.

Soldiers are armed with various weapons, which include the German GewehrK98K Mauser rifle, MP38 machine pistol, and MG 34 light machine gun, and the Russian Mosin Nagant M1891/30 rifle, PPSh-41 machine pistol, and DP 1928 light machine gun.  Each weapon has its own characteristics for range, penetration, effectiveness, and reliability.  Enough men are supplied (33 for each side) to fit out squads of many different varieties.

Supplied scenarios include:  Clash of Squads (CL1), Meeting Engagement (CL2), Hotel Henn (CL3), Scamble at Dusk (CL4), Reconnaissance in Force (CL5), and Breakthrough (CL6).  Each scenario has its own victory conditions, special rules, and play variants.

The game comes complete with Map 1 & Map 2 (8.5"x11" each),  German counter set A (60 counters & markers), Russian counter set B (60 counters & markers), two identical double-sided Reference Cards, three double-sided Scenario Cards (with six scenarios), and 12-page illustrated rule booklet.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

boardgame: Stamford Bridge: End of the Viking Age + A Hill Near Hastings
description:
Appearing in the first issue of Yaah! magazine, Stamford Bridge: End of the Viking Age is a low-complexity two-player wargame about the 25 September 1066 Battle of Stamford Bridge. Three weeks before his defeat at Hastings, King Harold Godwinson surprised his brother Tostig and King Harald Hardrada of Norway and won a decisive, if costly victory against the Vikings.

At the heart of the game is a simple turn structure in which players choose two phases to perform: Retreat, Shield Wall, Move, or Combat. Players can also perform two Move Phases in a single turn, two separate Combat phases, or a more powerful-- if bloody-- Pitched Combat. Combat resolution is quick and accurately represents the brutal, costly nature of linear warfare of the period.

As leadership of the Viking forces passes from one leader to another, the rules of the game are changed, imparting a sense of historical narrative while giving both players an equal chance of victory.
